Runtime-backed framework for diagnostic playbooks, evidence, and support workflows.

# AlgoSec Diagnostic Framework Support Cockpit is the current interaction model for the Starlight publisher.

Live baseline

algosec-lab on algosec running Rocky Linux 8.10 (Green Obsidian).

Observed scope

263 services, 57 listeners, 114 config checkpoints, 109 log checkpoints.

## First response

How to use this site

  • Confirm the customer appliance hostname, OS family, and any visible build markers against the lab baseline.
  • Check whether the top AlgoSec services are active and enabled in the customer environment.
  • Compare Apache-routed endpoints and local listener ports before moving into deeper service-specific diagnosis.
  • Use the recorded config and log checkpoints for the top services before inferring broader platform issues.

Why this shape

  • Quick jump stays visible while you read lower command content.
  • Each checkpoint stays collapsed until you need it.
  • The JSON baseline stays canonical. This page is only a render.
## Playbooks

ui-and-proxy

ASMS UI is down services: httpd.service, keycloak.service, ms-metro.service Check whether the host is under storage, inode, memory, or CPU pressure before blaming the ASMS UI path. Confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route toward the auth and app branches.

core-aff

FireFlow Backend paths: aff-boot; services: aff-boot.service, algosec-dfs.service, postgresql.service Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989. Confirm postgresql.service is active before treating FireFlow symptoms as application-only.

microservice-platform

Microservice Platform paths: ms-batch-application, ms-bflow, ms-cloudlicensing, ms-configuration; services: algobot-microsoft-teams.service, algobot-slack.service, algobot-webex-teams.service, algosec-ms.service, elastic-ssh-tunnel.service, httpd.service Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and the top ms-* services are active. Compare the routed microservice paths first: ms-batch-application, ms-bflow, ms-cloudlicensing.

messaging-and-data

Messaging and Data services: activemq.service, postgresql.service Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present. Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy.

Decision rule

If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.

Likely services

activemq.service postgresql.service

Command-first flow

Open one checkpoint, run the listed read-only commands, compare the healthy signal, then stop at the first failure point.

Quick jump

Step 1 Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue. Step 2 Confirm activemq Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present before assuming the issue is only downstream data state. Step 3 Confirm postgresql Confirm postgresql.service is active and that the customer environment shows no obvious database connectivity or state error. Step 4 Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain.

Symptoms that fit here

  • Job stuck or not progressing
  • Data-backed action failing
Step 1 Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue.

If healthy: Continue to broker validation.

If this fails: If the symptom is UI-only, switch to UI and Proxy or Microservice Platform instead of staying here.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
Step 2 Confirm activemq Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present before assuming the issue is only downstream data state.

Check activemq.service status

Run

```bash systemctl status activemq.service --no-pager ```

Verification: Unit is loaded and active (running).

Linux note: service status
- This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now. - `Loaded` means the service unit exists on the server. - `Active (running)` means the service is up now. `Main PID` means systemd still sees the main process.

If not healthy: If the broker is not active, stop here and treat messaging infrastructure as the likely failure point.

Check broker listener

Run

```bash ss -lntp | grep -E ':61616\b' ```

Verification: A listening socket is present for 61616.

Linux note: listening port
- This checks whether Linux is listening on the expected port. - `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections. - If the port is missing, the service may be down, slow to start, or bound to the wrong place.

If not healthy: If 61616 is missing, queued work may fail before any feature-specific processing begins.

If healthy: Continue to database validation.

If this fails: Treat activemq.service or the 61616 listener as the current failure point.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
  • Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
Step 3 Confirm postgresql Confirm postgresql.service is active and that the customer environment shows no obvious database connectivity or state error.

Check postgresql.service status

Run

```bash systemctl status postgresql.service --no-pager ```

Verification: Unit is loaded and active (running).

Linux note: service status
- This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now. - `Loaded` means the service unit exists on the server. - `Active (running)` means the service is up now. `Main PID` means systemd still sees the main process.

If not healthy: If the database is not active, treat data availability as the underlying failure point.

If healthy: Continue to backlog or queue-state review.

If this fails: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this issue.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
  • Any stuck job, queue, or backlog indicator shown during the customer session.
Step 4 Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain.

Review activemq.service logs

Run

```bash journalctl -u activemq.service -n 50 --no-pager ```

Verification: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.

Linux note: service logs
- Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue. - Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries. - Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.

If not healthy: Use broker log lines to decide whether the queue layer is blocked, degraded, or forwarding the issue downstream.

Review postgresql.service logs

Run

```bash journalctl -u postgresql.service -n 50 --no-pager ```

Verification: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.

Linux note: service logs
- Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue. - Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries. - Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.

If not healthy: Use database log lines to spot deeper availability or permission problems affecting queued work.

If healthy: If infrastructure checks passed, move to the feature-specific domain that owns the stalled job or action.

If this fails: If queue or backlog evidence cannot be gathered, escalate with the broker and database checks already captured.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
  • Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
  • Any stuck job, queue, or backlog indicator shown during the customer session.

Step 1 Identify the failing FireFlow action and confirm that the symptom is tied to /FireFlow/api or /aff/api Identify the failing FireFlow action and confirm that the symptom is tied to /FireFlow/api or /aff/api.

Check config mapping

Run

```bash grep -n 1989 /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf ```

Verification: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.

Linux note: config check
- This checks whether the expected mapping still exists in the service config. - Use it to confirm the route, port, or target value is still what the application expects.

If not healthy: Use the known proxy mapping to confirm the FireFlow path really terminates at the expected backend.

If healthy: Continue to aff-boot and route validation.

If this fails: If the symptom is not tied to FireFlow, switch to a different domain playbook instead of staying here.

Collect before moving on:

  • Result of the failing FireFlow action, including the exact endpoint or screen.
Step 2 Confirm aff-boot Confirm aff-boot.service is active and that the failing FireFlow route still proxies to localhost:1989.

Check aff-boot.service status

Run

```bash systemctl status aff-boot.service --no-pager ```

Verification: Unit is loaded and active (running).

Linux note: service status
- This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now. - `Loaded` means the service unit exists on the server. - `Active (running)` means the service is up now. `Main PID` means systemd still sees the main process.

If not healthy: If aff-boot is not active, stop here and treat the FireFlow service itself as the failure point.

Check FireFlow listener

Run

```bash ss -lntp | grep -E ':1989\b' ```

Verification: A listening socket is present for 1989.

Linux note: listening port
- This checks whether Linux is listening on the expected port. - `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections. - If the port is missing, the service may be down, slow to start, or bound to the wrong place.

If not healthy: If the FireFlow listener is missing, the backend route has nothing healthy to terminate to.

Check config mapping

Run

```bash grep -n 1989 /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf ```

Verification: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.

Linux note: config check
- This checks whether the expected mapping still exists in the service config. - Use it to confirm the route, port, or target value is still what the application expects.

If not healthy: If the proxy config no longer points to 1989, the FireFlow request path may be broken even when the service is up.

If healthy: Continue to database validation.

If this fails: Treat aff-boot.service or the 1989 route as the current failure point.

Collect before moving on:

  • Result of the failing FireFlow action, including the exact endpoint or screen.
  • Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
Step 3 Confirm postgresql Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy before assuming a FireFlow-only fault.

- This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now. - `Loaded` means the service unit exists on the server. - `Active (running)` means the service is up now. `Main PID` means systemd still sees the main process.

If not healthy: If the database is not active, FireFlow symptoms are likely downstream of data availability.

If healthy: Continue to FireFlow log review.

If this fails: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this customer issue.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
  • Any recent FireFlow-related lines from the customer-facing logs or service status output.
Step 4 Collect the latest FireFlow-related error lines or status output and decide whether the failure stays in FireFlow logic or must move deeper into dependencies Collect the latest FireFlow-related error lines or status output and decide whether the failure stays in FireFlow logic or must move deeper into dependencies.

Verification: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.

Linux note: service logs
- Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue. - Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries. - Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.

If not healthy: Use recent log lines to separate local service failure, permission issues, and deeper shared dependency problems.

If healthy: Inspect dependency services next: httpd.service, ms-configuration.service.

If this fails: If the error evidence cannot be collected, escalate with the routed-path and service-state checks already captured.

Collect before moving on:

  • Visible state of algosec-ms.service and ms-configuration.service.
  • Any service status, console error, or log snippet for the failing ms-* feature path.

Use this when the ASMS UI is down and the engineer is checking the appliance from SSH.

Use this when the ASMS UI is down and the engineer is checking the appliance from SSH. ## ASMS UI Working Map

Working rule

Start at the host, move into Apache/HTTPD serving the UI, then split into auth and app branches. Stop at the first place where useful work no longer happens.

What this page is proving

The goal is not to prove that services merely exist. The goal is to prove whether the ASMS UI path can do useful work for the current customer scenario.

Useful-work path

  1. Step 1 Host can support useful work Start here. Check storage, inode, memory, OOM, load, and CPU pressure to decide whether the Linux host can still support Apache, Keycloak, Metro, logging, and temp-file writes.
  2. Step 2 Apache/HTTPD serving the UI If the host looks healthy, confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route requests into the auth and app branches.
  3. Step 3 Auth handoff can do useful work Check whether the legacy auth-trigger hop reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then confirm the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff without pretending they are the first observed neighbor.
  4. Step 4 App branch can do useful work Check whether ms-metro is serving useful app traffic and showing healthy JVM behavior, not only whether port 8080 is open.
  5. Step 5 Useful work stops here If the host and both branches still look up, use Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, and Metro clues to find the first clear stop point.

Quick jump

Step 1 Host can support useful work Start here. Check storage, inode, memory, OOM, load, and CPU pressure to decide whether the Linux host can still support Apache, Keycloak, Metro, logging, and temp-file writes. Step 2 Apache/HTTPD serving the UI If the host looks healthy, confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route requests into the auth and app branches. Step 3 Auth handoff can do useful work Check whether the legacy auth-trigger hop reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then confirm the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff without pretending they are the first observed neighbor. Step 4 App branch can do useful work Check whether ms-metro is serving useful app traffic and showing healthy JVM behavior, not only whether port 8080 is open. Step 5 Useful work stops here If the host and both branches still look up, use Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, and Metro clues to find the first clear stop point.

Symptoms that fit here

  • ASMS UI is down
  • ASMS login page not loading
Step 1 Host can support useful work Check whether the host can support useful work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm storage, inode, memory, load, OOM, and CPU pressure are not already blocking Apache, Keycloak, Metro, log writing, or temp-file work.

Why this checkpoint exists

Every major playbook starts with the Linux host. The first question is not whether a number looks high. It is whether current host pressure is already enough to break useful work in the UI system.

Check storage pressure on runtime filesystems

Run

```bash df -h ```

Verification: Runtime filesystems still have enough free space for logs, temp files, and service work.

Linux note: disk pressure
- This checks human-readable disk usage for the main filesystems. - Focus on `Use%` and `Avail`, especially for `/` and `/data`. - Disk pressure means the filesystem is close to full. When that happens, services can fail to write logs, temp files, or runtime data.

Look for: Use% below 100%, Avail is not 0, / and /data still have free space

If not healthy: If /, /boot, or /data is close to full, Apache, Keycloak, Metro, installers, and log writers can all fail or behave unpredictably.

Known working example

```text Filesystem Size Used Avail Use% Mounted on /dev/mapper/rl-root 60G 16G 45G 27% / /dev/mapper/rl-data 238G 21G 218G 9% /data ```

Check inode pressure on runtime filesystems

Run

```bash df -ih ```

Verification: Runtime filesystems still have free inodes for logs, temp files, sockets, and service output.

Linux note: inode pressure
- This checks inode usage, which is different from normal disk space. - A filesystem can still have free space but fail because it has no free inodes left. - Inode pressure means the server has too many files or directory entries. Focus on `IUse%` and whether `IFree` is close to zero.

Look for: IUse% below 100%, IFree is not 0, / and /data still have free inodes

If not healthy: If inode use is high, the host can look like it has free disk while Apache, Keycloak, Metro, or log rotation still fail to create files.

Known working example

```text Filesystem Inodes IUsed IFree IUse% Mounted on /dev/mapper/rl-root 30M 343K 30M 2% / /dev/mapper/rl-data 119M 21K 119M 1% /data ```

Check memory pressure on JVM-backed services

Run

```bash free -h ```

Verification: Available memory is still present and swap is not carrying active pressure for the host.

Linux note: memory pressure
- This is the quick memory check for the host. - Focus on `available` memory and whether swap is starting to grow heavily. - Memory pressure means the server is low on available memory. When that happens, the server slows down, swap grows, or Linux may kill a process to protect itself.

Look for: available memory is present, swap is not exhausted, Mem and Swap are shown in GiB

If not healthy: If available memory is very low or swap is heavily used, Java services like Keycloak and Metro may slow down, hang, fail health checks, or get killed later.

Known working example

```text total used free shared buff/cache available Mem: 32Gi 13Gi 8.6Gi 2.4Gi 9Gi 15Gi Swap: 24Gi 0B 24Gi ```

Check current host load pressure

Run

```bash uptime ```

Verification: Load is not unexpectedly high for the host and does not already explain the UI symptom.

Linux note: system load
- This is the quick load check for the host. - Focus on the `load average` values and whether they look unexpectedly high for the server. - High load can mean CPU pressure, blocked work, or heavy I/O wait.

Look for: load average:, load values are not unexpectedly high

If not healthy: If load is unusually high, the system may be under CPU pressure, blocked work, or heavy I/O wait before you even reach the UI-specific branches.

Known working example

```text 14:42:03 up 17 days, 1:52, 1 user, load average: 0.25, 0.18, 0.11 ```

Check recent memory-kill pressure

Run

```bash journalctl -k --since "24 hours ago" --no-pager | grep -i -E "out of memory|oom|killed process" ```

Verification: No recent Out Of Memory lines are returned.

Linux note: OOM pressure
- This checks the Linux kernel log for memory-pressure kills. - OOM means Out Of Memory. Linux may kill a process to protect the server. - If you see OOM or `Killed process` lines, memory pressure is likely part of the failure.

Look for: No output is normal, No 'Out of memory' lines, No 'Killed process' lines

If not healthy: If OOM lines appear, the host has already been killing or starving processes under memory pressure, so downstream UI symptoms may only be the visible side effect.

Known working example

```text No output ```

Check which process owns CPU pressure right now

Run

```bash ps -eo pid,comm,%cpu,%mem --sort=-%cpu | head -n 10 ```

Verification: No unexpected process is consuming enough CPU to starve the rest of the ASMS path.

Linux note: top CPU consumers
- This shows which processes are using the most CPU right now. - Focus on whether one process is dominating CPU and whether that process matches the current symptom. - A single runaway process can starve the rest of the server and make higher-level application failures look worse than they are.

Look for: PID, COMMAND, %CPU, %MEM

If not healthy: If one process is consuming most of the CPU, treat that process as part of the current system pressure story before assuming the UI path itself is the root cause.

Known working example

```text PID COMMAND %CPU %MEM 6012 java 8.1 7.4 1018 httpd 1.2 0.4 3758 algosec_keycloa 0.8 1.6 ```
Step 2 Apache/HTTPD serving the UI Check whether Apache/HTTPD can do useful edge work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm httpd.service is running, 80 and 443 are listening, the suite login route still lands on the Apache-served UI page, representative /algosec-ui/ assets still load through Apache, and the proxy mapping still points toward the auth and app branches.

Why this checkpoint exists

After host health, Apache/HTTPD is the system edge for the UI path. The real question is whether the edge can still serve browser-useful login content and hand auth and app requests to the right neighbors, not only whether a process exists.

Check httpd.service status

Run

```bash systemctl status httpd.service --no-pager ```

Verification: Unit is loaded and active (running).

Linux note: service status
- This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now. - `Loaded` means the service unit exists on the server. - `Active (running)` means the service is up now. `Main PID` means systemd still sees the main process.

Look for: Loaded: loaded, Active: active (running), Main PID:, /usr/sbin/httpd

If not healthy: If this is not active, treat the edge service itself as the failure point before going deeper.

Known working example

```text ● httpd.service - The Apache HTTP Server Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/httpd.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled) Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:37:12 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago Main PID: 1018 (/usr/sbin/httpd) ```

Check edge listeners

Run

```bash ss -lntp | grep -E ':(80|443)\b' ```

Verification: A listening socket is present for 80, 443.

Linux note: listening port
- This checks whether Linux is listening on the expected port. - `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections. - If the port is missing, the service may be down, slow to start, or bound to the wrong place.

Look for: LISTEN, :80, :443, httpd

If not healthy: If 80/443 are missing, the UI path is failing at the listener or bind layer.

Known working example

```text LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:443 0.0.0.0:* users:(("/usr/sbin/httpd",pid=1018,fd=4),...) LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:80 0.0.0.0:* users:(("/usr/sbin/httpd",pid=1018,fd=3),...) ```

Check the legacy suite login route

Run

```bash curl -k -I --max-time 5 https://127.0.0.1/algosec/suite/login ```

Verification: Apache returns a redirect from the legacy suite login path into the current UI login page.

Linux note
- This command gives a focused check for the current step. - Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 302, Server: Apache, Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login

If not healthy: If the suite login route stops redirecting here, the edge path is already broken before Keycloak or Metro have a chance to help.

Known working example

```text HTTP/1.1 302 Found Server: Apache Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login ```

Check the Apache-served UI login page

Run

```bash curl -k -I --max-time 5 https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login ```

Verification: Apache returns the current UI login page with HTTP 200.

Linux note
- This command gives a focused check for the current step. - Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, Server: Apache, Content-Type: text/html

If not healthy: If Apache cannot serve the login page here, stop at the edge before chasing the auth or app neighbors.

Known working example

```text HTTP/1.1 200 OK Server: Apache Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 ```

Check representative /algosec-ui/ assets through Apache

Run

```bash for path in /algosec-ui/styles.css /algosec-ui/runtime.js /algosec-ui/main.js; do echo "=== $path ==="; curl -k -sS -D - -o /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out "https://127.0.0.1$path" | sed -n '1,8p'; printf 'BODY_BYTES '; wc -c < /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out; done ```

Verification: Apache returns HTTP 200 for representative CSS and JS assets and each body is non-empty.

Linux note: browser-useful edge assets
- This checks whether Apache is serving real UI assets, not only the login HTML shell. - Non-empty CSS and JavaScript bodies are a stronger edge proof because a browser needs them before sign-in can do useful work. - If the HTML is up but these assets fail, stay on Apache and the static UI path before chasing Keycloak or Metro.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, Server: Apache, Content-Type: text/css, Content-Type: application/javascript, BODY_BYTES

If not healthy: If the login HTML works but these assets fail, return the wrong type, or come back empty, the edge is not yet browser-useful even before Keycloak or Metro are checked.

Known working example

```text === /algosec-ui/styles.css === HTTP/1.1 200 OK Server: Apache Content-Type: text/css BODY_BYTES 758180 === /algosec-ui/runtime.js === HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/javascript BODY_BYTES 2737 ```

Check Apache routes for login, auth, and app branches

Run

```bash grep -R -n -E 'algosec-ui|algosec/suite|ProxyPass|ProxyPassReverse|keycloak|8443|afa/api/v1|8080' /etc/httpd/conf.d 2>/dev/null ```

Verification: Apache config still shows the login redirect, the UI alias, and the auth and app route mapping needed by the ASMS UI path.

Linux note
- This command gives a focused check for the current step. - Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.

Look for: algosec/suite, algosec-ui, ProxyPass, keycloak, 8443, afa/api/v1, 8080

If not healthy: If the expected proxy targets are missing or wrong, the edge can look healthy while routing useful work to the wrong place or nowhere useful.

Known working example

```text 78:RewriteRule "^/algosec/suite/login(.*)$" "/algosec-ui/login" [R] 299:AliasMatch (?i)^/algosec-ui/(.*)$ /usr/share/fa/suite/client/app/suite-new-ui/$1 1: 2: ProxyPass https://localhost:8443/ timeout=300 139: 140: ProxyPass http://localhost:8080/afa/api/v1 timeout=18000 retry=0 ```
Step 3 Auth handoff can do useful work Check the first auth-triggering chain after the static shell. On this lab, the browser first touches legacy suite setup and session-validation paths, then reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then proxied Keycloak token paths and FireFlow auth checks before the final handoff into `/afa/php/home.php`.

Why this checkpoint exists

The static shell alone does not prove the real auth order. On this lab the first JS-triggered request after the shell was `/seikan/login/setup`, the first session-validation hop was `POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false`, BusinessFlow first appeared as a redirect and later `POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login`, and FireFlow showed up as auth checks during sign-in plus `FireFlowBridge.js` after the PHP home page loaded.

Check the shipped login setup endpoint first

Run

```bash curl -k -sS -D - https://127.0.0.1/seikan/login/setup -o /tmp/asms-login-setup.json && sed -n '1,40p' /tmp/asms-login-setup.json ```

Verification: The setup endpoint returns JSON that shows the current login mode and whether SSO is enabled on this appliance.

Linux note: login setup mode
- This checks the login setup endpoint that the shipped UI uses before credentials are submitted. - Use it to see whether this appliance is advertising SSO or a different login mode for the current journey. - If `isSSOEnabled` is false here, do not assume Keycloak is the first observed auth hop without stronger request evidence.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, Server: Apache-Coyote, "isSSOEnabled" : false, "needsFirstTimeSetup" : false

If not healthy: If this endpoint fails, the first auth-trigger path is already broken before credentials are submitted. If it shows `"isSSOEnabled" : false`, do not assume Keycloak is the first real auth hop for this journey.

Known working example

```text HTTP/1.1 200 Server: Apache-Coyote { "isSSOEnabled" : false, "needsFirstTimeSetup" : false, "isAffEnabled" : true, ```

Check the legacy suite session-validation hop with cookies

Run

```bash rm -f /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers /tmp/asms-auth-hop.body; curl -k -sS -L -c /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies -b /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies -A 'Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/123.0.0.0 Safari/537.36' -H 'Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8' -H 'Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9' -H 'Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1' -D /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers -o /tmp/asms-auth-hop.body https://127.0.0.1/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php; sed -n '1,20p' /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers; echo '--- cookies ---'; cat /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies 2>/dev/null ```

Verification: The legacy session-validation path either redirects back to `/algosec-ui/login` and sets a PHP session cookie, or returns a different concrete auth clue for the same reproduced journey.

Linux note: legacy session validation
- This checks a legacy suite login endpoint with a cookie jar so one reproduced journey stays connected. - A redirect back to `/algosec-ui/login` with a new PHP session cookie is a real auth-path clue, even without credentials. - If this path lights up before `/BusinessFlow` or `/keycloak/`, treat it as the first observed auth-trigger hop for this journey and keep the later named modules behind it.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 302, Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=, Location: /algosec-ui/login?last_url=%2Fafa%2Fphp%2FSuiteLoginSessionValidation.php

If not healthy: If this path is the first auth-triggering hop you can actually reproduce, treat it as stronger evidence than a guessed Keycloak-first model. On this lab the browser hit `POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false` before the later Keycloak and BusinessFlow login activity. If it never appears in the reproduced journey, keep it inferred only.

Known working example

```text HTTP/1.1 302 Found Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=pp3eujujutk0t1mrmvfd6jflrm; path=/; secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax Location: /algosec-ui/login?last_url=%2Fafa%2Fphp%2FSuiteLoginSessionValidation.php ```

Check the BusinessFlow checkpoint through Apache

Run

```bash curl -k -sS -D - https://127.0.0.1/BusinessFlow/shallow_health_check -o /tmp/asms-bflow-shallow.json && sed -n '1,20p' /tmp/asms-bflow-shallow.json; echo '---'; curl -k -sS -D - https://127.0.0.1/BusinessFlow/deep_health_check -o /tmp/asms-bflow-deep.json && sed -n '1,40p' /tmp/asms-bflow-deep.json ```

Verification: BusinessFlow health endpoints return HTTP 200 and show that the local BusinessFlow, AFA, and AFF links are still healthy enough to support the login handoff.

Linux note: BusinessFlow checkpoint
- This checks the first named operational module we have observed in the customer-visible ASMS login path. - Use the shallow check to prove BusinessFlow is answering through Apache, then the deep check to confirm its AFA, AFF, and database links still look healthy. - If these checks fail after the legacy setup hop worked, stop on the BusinessFlow checkpoint before blaming Keycloak or Metro.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, "status":true, "name":"ABF connection", "name":"AFA connection", "name":"AFF connection"

If not healthy: Use this after the legacy session-validation hop reaches the first named module. On this lab BusinessFlow appeared before the later Keycloak and FireFlow auth checks, so a failing BusinessFlow health response is a real stop point, not just background noise.

Known working example

```text HTTP/1.1 200 OK {"healthChecks":[{"name":"ABF connection","status":true,"information":"BusinessFlow"}],"status":true} --- HTTP/1.1 200 OK {"healthChecks":[{"name":"Postgres connection","status":true,"information":"postgres connection achieved"},{"name":"AFA connection","status":true,"information":"Firewall Analyzer"},{"name":"AFF connection","status":true,"information":"FireFlow"}],"status":true} ```

Check the FireFlow auth-coupled signals

Run

```bash curl -k -sS -D - 'https://127.0.0.1/FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/?login=1' -o /tmp/asms-fireflow-auth.body && sed -n '1,20p' /tmp/asms-fireflow-auth.body; echo '--- apache ---'; grep -E '/FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/|/FireFlow/XML/CommandsDispatcher|FireFlowBridge.js' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 20 ```

Verification: FireFlow auth checks return HTTP 200 and recent Apache lines show CheckAuthentication, CommandsDispatcher, or FireFlowBridge activity in the same customer-visible login minute.

Linux note: FireFlow auth bridge
- This checks the FireFlow signals that appear during sign-in and immediately after the PHP home page loads. - Treat FireFlow here as an auth-coupled module, not as the first branch in the login journey. - If these checks fail while BusinessFlow and Keycloak looked healthy, the stop point is later in the authenticated handoff, not at the static shell.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, Set-Cookie: RT_SID_FireFlow.443=, /FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/, FireFlowBridge.js

If not healthy: Keep FireFlow inside the same auth handoff for now. On this lab it did not show up as the first named module, but it did appear during sign-in and immediately after `/afa/php/home.php`, which makes it a support-useful checkpoint when the browser reaches home and then stalls.

Known working example

ASMS UI Working Map

Section titled “ASMS UI Working Map”

Working rule

Start at the host, move into Apache/HTTPD serving the UI, then split into auth and app branches. Stop at the first place where useful work no longer happens.

What this page is proving

The goal is not to prove that services merely exist. The goal is to prove whether the ASMS UI path can do useful work for the current customer scenario.

Useful-work path

  1. Step 1 Host can support useful work Start here. Check storage, inode, memory, OOM, load, and CPU pressure to decide whether the Linux host can still support Apache, Keycloak, Metro, logging, and temp-file writes.
  2. Step 2 Apache/HTTPD serving the UI If the host looks healthy, confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route requests into the auth and app branches.
  3. Step 3 Auth handoff can do useful work Check whether the legacy auth-trigger hop reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then confirm the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff without pretending they are the first observed neighbor.
  4. Step 4 App branch can do useful work Check whether ms-metro is serving useful app traffic and showing healthy JVM behavior, not only whether port 8080 is open.
  5. Step 5 Useful work stops here If the host and both branches still look up, use Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, and Metro clues to find the first clear stop point.

Symptoms that fit here

  • ASMS UI is down
  • ASMS login page not loading
Step 1 Host can support useful work Check whether the host can support useful work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm storage, inode, memory, load, OOM, and CPU pressure are not already blocking Apache, Keycloak, Metro, log writing, or temp-file work.

Why this checkpoint exists

Every major playbook starts with the Linux host. The first question is not whether a number looks high. It is whether current host pressure is already enough to break useful work in the UI system.

Check storage pressure on runtime filesystems

Run

Verification: Runtime filesystems still have enough free space for logs, temp files, and service work.

Linux note: disk pressure
  • This checks human-readable disk usage for the main filesystems.
  • Focus on Use% and Avail, especially for / and /data.
  • Disk pressure means the filesystem is close to full. When that happens, services can fail to write logs, temp files, or runtime data.

Look for: Use% below 100%, Avail is not 0, / and /data still have free space

If not healthy: If /, /boot, or /data is close to full, Apache, Keycloak, Metro, installers, and log writers can all fail or behave unpredictably.

Known working example

Check inode pressure on runtime filesystems

Run

Verification: Runtime filesystems still have free inodes for logs, temp files, sockets, and service output.

Linux note: inode pressure
  • This checks inode usage, which is different from normal disk space.
  • A filesystem can still have free space but fail because it has no free inodes left.
  • Inode pressure means the server has too many files or directory entries. Focus on IUse% and whether IFree is close to zero.

Look for: IUse% below 100%, IFree is not 0, / and /data still have free inodes

If not healthy: If inode use is high, the host can look like it has free disk while Apache, Keycloak, Metro, or log rotation still fail to create files.

Known working example

Check memory pressure on JVM-backed services

Run

Verification: Available memory is still present and swap is not carrying active pressure for the host.

Linux note: memory pressure
  • This is the quick memory check for the host.
  • Focus on available memory and whether swap is starting to grow heavily.
  • Memory pressure means the server is low on available memory. When that happens, the server slows down, swap grows, or Linux may kill a process to protect itself.

Look for: available memory is present, swap is not exhausted, Mem and Swap are shown in GiB

If not healthy: If available memory is very low or swap is heavily used, Java services like Keycloak and Metro may slow down, hang, fail health checks, or get killed later.

Known working example

Check current host load pressure

Run

Verification: Load is not unexpectedly high for the host and does not already explain the UI symptom.

Linux note: system load
  • This is the quick load check for the host.
  • Focus on the load average values and whether they look unexpectedly high for the server.
  • High load can mean CPU pressure, blocked work, or heavy I/O wait.

Look for: load average:, load values are not unexpectedly high

If not healthy: If load is unusually high, the system may be under CPU pressure, blocked work, or heavy I/O wait before you even reach the UI-specific branches.

Known working example

Check recent memory-kill pressure

Run

Verification: No recent Out Of Memory lines are returned.

Linux note: OOM pressure
  • This checks the Linux kernel log for memory-pressure kills.
  • OOM means Out Of Memory. Linux may kill a process to protect the server.
  • If you see OOM or Killed process lines, memory pressure is likely part of the failure.

Look for: No output is normal, No 'Out of memory' lines, No 'Killed process' lines

If not healthy: If OOM lines appear, the host has already been killing or starving processes under memory pressure, so downstream UI symptoms may only be the visible side effect.

Known working example

Check which process owns CPU pressure right now

Run

Verification: No unexpected process is consuming enough CPU to starve the rest of the ASMS path.

Linux note: top CPU consumers
  • This shows which processes are using the most CPU right now.
  • Focus on whether one process is dominating CPU and whether that process matches the current symptom.
  • A single runaway process can starve the rest of the server and make higher-level application failures look worse than they are.

Look for: PID, COMMAND, %CPU, %MEM

Step 2 Apache/HTTPD serving the UI Check whether Apache/HTTPD can do useful edge work for the ASMS UI path. Why this checkpoint exists

After host health, Apache/HTTPD is the system edge for the UI path. The real question is whether the edge can still serve browser-useful login content and hand auth and app requests to the right neighbors, not only whether a process exists.

Run

Linux note: service status
  • This asks systemd whether the service is known and running now.
  • Loaded means the service unit exists on the server.
  • Active (running) means the service is up now. Main PID means systemd still sees the main process.

Look for: Loaded: loaded, Active: active (running), Main PID:, /usr/sbin/httpd

If not healthy: If this is not active, treat the edge service itself as the failure point before going deeper.

Step 3 Auth handoff can do useful work Check the first auth-triggering chain after the static shell. On this lab, the browser first touches legacy suite setup and session-validation paths, then reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then proxied Keycloak token paths and FireFlow auth checks before the final handoff into `/afa/php/home.php`.

Why this checkpoint exists

The static shell alone does not prove the real auth order. On this lab the first JS-triggered request after the shell was `/seikan/login/setup`, the first session-validation hop was `POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false`, BusinessFlow first appeared as a redirect and later `POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login`, and FireFlow showed up as auth checks during sign-in plus `FireFlowBridge.js` after the PHP home page loaded.

Check the shipped login setup endpoint first

Run

Verification: The setup endpoint returns JSON that shows the current login mode and whether SSO is enabled on this appliance.

Linux note: login setup mode
  • This checks the login setup endpoint that the shipped UI uses before credentials are submitted.
  • Use it to see whether this appliance is advertising SSO or a different login mode for the current journey.
  • If isSSOEnabled is false here, do not assume Keycloak is the first observed auth hop without stronger request evidence.

Look for: HTTP/1.1 200, Server: Apache-Coyote, "isSSOEnabled" : false, "needsFirstTimeSetup" : false

If not healthy: If this endpoint fails, the first auth-trigger path is already broken before credentials are submitted. If it shows `"isSSOEnabled" : false`, do not assume Keycloak is the first real auth hop for this journey.

If it never appears in the reproduced journey, keep it inferred only.

Step 4 App branch can do useful work Check whether the app branch can do useful work after the authenticated handoff. After login reaches `/afa/php/home.php`, treat the rendered home page itself as the main signal and use the immediate home-shell follow-ups and light Metro refresh traffic as supporting clues before assuming a broader subsystem failure.

Why this checkpoint exists

The app branch can fail even when Apache/HTTPD and the auth chain look healthy. On this lab the first post-home activity was mostly home-shell wiring plus light Metro refresh traffic, not an immediate fan-out into every subsystem. The earliest observed follow-ups after `/afa/php/home.php` were `FireFlowBridge.js`, `logo.php`, `GET /afa/getStatus`, `POST /afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh`, and `GET /afa/api/v1/license`. In a bounded client-side check, blocking `FireFlowBridge.js` did not stop the home page from rendering with normal menu and summary text, so these post-home routes are better treated as supporting clues than as first-class gates for initial home-page availability.
